title: Natural floor cleaner
subtitle: A diluted castile soap solution with lavender for hard floors
excerpt: A gentle hard-floor cleaner made from liquid castile soap diluted in warm water with lavender essential oil. Suitable for sealed wooden floors, laminate, vinyl, and ceramic tiles. Made fresh per mop load; this recipe gives the concentrate to dilute into a standard mop bucket.
sourceNotes: Castile soap dilution guidance for floor cleaning, Dr. Bronner's usage guide (drbronner.com, May 2026). Essential Oil Safety, Tisserand and Young (2014), lavender in rinse-off cleaning products.

content.text: A natural floor cleaner. Liquid castile soap at a 0.5% dilution (half a teaspoon per 5 litres of water) is sufficient to lift general foot-traffic grime from hard floors without leaving a soapy residue. At higher concentrations castile soap leaves a slippery film that attracts more dirt; less is more here. Lavender essential oil adds fragrance and contributes a mild antimicrobial note at use concentration.

Do not mix castile soap with white vinegar in the same bucket. Castile soap is alkaline; vinegar is acidic. They neutralise each other, turning the soap into an oily, scum-like residue that leaves a haze on floors rather than cleaning them. Use one or the other; castile for greasy grime, vinegar for mineral deposits and general sanitising.

items.name: Castile liquid soap
items.prepNote: concentrated liquid castile soap. Added to 5 litres of warm water in the mop bucket. This is the measure for one standard mop bucket.

items.name: Lavender essential oil
items.prepNote: Lavandula angustifolia lavender essential oil. Added to the mop bucket. Fragrance and mild antimicrobial note at use concentration. Omit for an unscented version.

content.text: Fill a 5-litre mop bucket with warm (not hot) water. Add the castile soap and the essential oil if using. Stir once or twice to distribute; do not agitate vigorously or the soap will foam excessively. Dip the mop, wring well (the mop should be damp, not dripping), and mop as usual. Change the bucket water if it becomes visibly dirty.

Safe for: sealed hardwood and engineered wood floors (damp mop only; standing water damages wood), laminate, vinyl, linoleum, and ceramic or porcelain tiles. Not safe for: unsealed wood or stone floors (water penetrates unsealed surfaces); waxed wood floors (the soap removes the wax finish over time). For natural stone tiles (marble, slate, limestone), use a pH-neutral stone cleaner rather than alkaline castile soap.
Make fresh and discard any unused diluted solution after each mopping session. The undiluted castile soap in its original bottle keeps for 3 years at room temperature.
